attributes.stem:
A 24-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a six-month history of progressive blurred vision, eye strain, and frequent headaches. She denies any nausea or weight loss. The symptoms occur at all viewing distances and are worse when reading or using digital devices, as well as nighttime with extensive glare from the lights. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She works as a graphic designer. Her grandfather had cataract surgery at age 69 years, and her mother and father both have glaucoma.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her visual acuity is 6/12 in both eyes. Pinhole testing improves her vision to 6/6. External eye examination reveals no abnormalities. The remainder of the neurological examination is normal.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?
options: Magnetic resonance imaging of the brain
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with classic symptoms of uncorrected astigmatism, including blurred vision at all distances, eye strain (particularly with near work), glare from night lights and headaches. The improvement with pinhole testing suggests a refractive error. It is a common condition, though the prevalence varies across countries. Regular eye checks from childhood can help to detect early signs of astigmatism to help prevent further visual difficulties and affects on education from poor vision.

Automated refraction is the most appropriate next step as it provides an objective measurement of refractive error, including astigmatism. This will be followed by subjective refraction to determine the exact prescription needed for correction. Keratometry, which is typically part of the automated refraction process, measures corneal curvature and helps quantify the degree and axis of astigmatism.

Magnetic resonance imaging of the brain would be appropriate if there were neurological symptoms or signs suggesting intracranial pathology. However, the symptoms are typical of a refractive error, and the normal neurological examination makes intracranial pathology unlikely.

Fundoscopy is important in a complete eye examination but would not be the first step in diagnosing astigmatism. It would be more appropriate if there were symptoms suggesting retinal pathology.

Visual field testing would be indicated if there were symptoms or signs suggesting visual field defects, such as those caused by glaucoma or neurological conditions. The symptoms here are consistent with a refractive error.

Optical coherence tomography is used to examine retinal structure and would not be the first step in investigating symptoms suggestive of a refractive error. It would be more appropriate in cases where retinal pathology is suspected.
learningPoints: Astigmatism commonly presents with blurred vision at all distances, headaches, and eye strain.
Diagnosis of astigmatism requires both subjective symptoms and objective findings on refraction.
Keratometry is a key diagnostic tool for measuring corneal curvature in astigmatism.
references: Remon L, Monsoriu J, Furlan W. Influence of different types of astigmatism on visual acuity. Journal of Optometry.  2017: 10 (3).  Available at: https://www.journalofoptometry.org/en-influence-different-types-astigmatism-on-articulo-S1888429616300498

Kanclerz, Piotr, Natasza Bazylczyk, and Carla Lanca. “The Prevalence of Astigmatism and Spectacle Wear in Polish Schoolchildren.” Journal of Binocular Vision and Ocular Motility. 2023: 73 (4): 124–30. Available at: https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/2576117X.2023.2237839#abstract
